title: BBQ-mIS: a parallel quantum algorithm for graph coloring problems
authors: Chiara Vercellino, Giacomo Vitali, Paolo Viviani, Edoardo Giusto, Alberto Scionti, Andrea Scarabosio, Olivier Terzo, Bartolomeo Montrucchio
abstract: Among the limitations of current quantum machines, the qubits count represents one of the most critical challenges for porting reasonably large computational problems, such as those coming from real-world applications, to the scale of the quantum hardware. In this regard, one possibility is to decompose the problems at hand and exploit parallelism over multiple size-limited quantum resources. To this purpose, we designed a hybrid quantum-classical algorithm, i.e., BBQ-mIS, to solve graph coloring problems on Rydberg atoms quantum machines. The BBQ-mIS algorithm combines the natural representation of Maximum Independent Set (MIS) problems onto the machine Hamiltonian with a Branch&Bound (BB) approach to identify a proper graph coloring. In the proposed solution, the graph representation emerges from qubit interactions (qubits represent vertexes of the graph), and the coloring is then retrieved by iteratively assigning one color to a maximal set of independent vertexes of the graph, still minimizing the number of colors with the Branch&Bound approach. We emulated real quantum hardware onto an IBM Power9-based cluster, with 32 cores/node and 256 GB/node, and exploited an MPI-enhanced library to implement the parallelism for the BBQ-mIS algorithm. Considering this use case, we also identify some technical requirements and challenges for an effective HPC-QC integration. The results show that our problem decomposition is effective in terms of graph coloring solutions quality, and provide a reference for applying this methodology to other quantum technologies or applications.
summary: The paper presents BBQ-mIS, a hybrid algorithm that uses classical Branch and Bound to decompose large graph coloring problems into smaller subproblems solvable on Rydberg atom quantum hardware. By parallelizing these tasks across multiple quantum resources, the method achieves optimal coloring solutions despite limited qubit counts. This work provides a blueprint for integrating quantum processing units with high-performance computing clusters.
main_problem: The challenge of solving large-scale graph coloring problems on current quantum hardware with limited qubit counts.
main_result: The BBQ-mIS algorithm effectively decomposes large graphs into smaller tasks, achieving coloring solutions that match the optimal results of the Gurobi classical solver.
method: A hybrid quantum-classical approach combining a classical Branch and Bound (BB) algorithm with a quantum routine (QAOA) to identify maximal independent sets.
model_or_system: The algorithm is designed for neutral atom quantum machines using the Rydberg blockade effect, where qubits represent vertices and edges are defined by the blockade radius.
key_observables: The occupancy of the Rydberg state (n_i) and the distribution of maximal independent set solutions.
important_parameters: Rydberg blockade radius (r_b), Rabi frequency (Omega), detuning (delta), and pulse sequence duration.
main_assumptions: The graph representation relies on a Unit-Disk Graph (UDG) model, which may limit generality for non-UDG structures.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the Branch and Bound node structure, pruning criteria, and priority score; Figure 2 compares the coloring performance of BBQ-mIS and Greedy-it-MIS against the Gurobi benchmark.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the graph coloring problem and hardware limitations, describes the BBQ-mIS algorithm and its hybrid architecture, details the Rydberg atom physical model and emulation setup, presents benchmarking results against classical solvers, and discusses HPC-QC integration challenges.
why_interesting: It demonstrates a practical strategy for using Rydberg-based quantum simulators to solve combinatorial optimization problems by leveraging the natural mapping of graphs to atom-atom interactions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Representation of the Branch&Bound scheme underlying the BBQ- mIS algorithm.

Fig. 2. Comparison of GC results: Greedy-it-MIS and BBQ-mIS algorithms are used to color all graphs in our dataset, Gurobi solver provides a benchmark for the coloring solution. The implementation for the MIS solver is based on the Pulser library.
